The **MIT Sloan School of Management** (branded as **MIT Sloan**) is the [business school](/wiki/Business_school "Business school") of the [Massachusetts Institute of Technology](/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology"), a [private university](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") in [Cambridge, Massachusetts](/wiki/Cambridge,_Massachusetts "Cambridge, Massachusetts").[[3]](#cite_note-about-mit-sloan-3)

MIT Sloan offers [bachelor's](/wiki/Bachelor%27s_degree "Bachelor's degree"), [master's](/wiki/Master%27s_degree "Master's degree"), and [doctoral](/wiki/Doctorate "Doctorate") degree programs, as well as [executive education](/wiki/Executive_education "Executive education").[[3]](#cite_note-about-mit-sloan-3) Many influential ideas in management and finance originated at the school, including the [Black–Scholes model](/wiki/Black%E2%80%93Scholes_model "Black–Scholes model"), the [random walk hypothesis](/wiki/Random_walk_hypothesis "Random walk hypothesis"), the [binomial options pricing model](/wiki/Binomial_options_pricing_model "Binomial options pricing model"), and the field of [system dynamics](/wiki/System_dynamics "System dynamics"). The faculty has included numerous [Nobel laureates in economics](/wiki/List_of_Nobel_laureates_in_Economics "List of Nobel laureates in Economics") and [John Bates Clark Medal](/wiki/John_Bates_Clark_Medal "John Bates Clark Medal") winners.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=MIT_Sloan_School_of_Management&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/MITSloanE62.jpg/250px-MITSloanE62.jpg)](/wiki/File:MITSloanE62.jpg)

MIT Sloan completed its new central building, known as *E62*, in 2010.

The MIT Sloan School of Management began in 1914 as the engineering administration curriculum ("Course 15") in the MIT Department of Economics and Statistics. The scope and depth of this educational focus grew steadily in response to advances in the theory and practice of management.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

A program offering a master's degree in management was established in 1925. The world's first university-based mid-career education program—the [Sloan Fellows](/wiki/Sloan_Fellows "Sloan Fellows") program—was created in 1931 under the sponsorship of [Alfred P. Sloan](/wiki/Alfred_P._Sloan "Alfred P. Sloan"), himself an 1895 MIT graduate, who was the chief executive officer of [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors_Corporation "General Motors Corporation") and has since been credited with creating the modern corporation.[[5]](#cite_note-5) An [Alfred P. Sloan Foundation](/wiki/Alfred_P._Sloan_Foundation "Alfred P. Sloan Foundation") grant established the MIT School of Industrial Management in 1952 with the charge of educating the "ideal manager". In 1964, the school was renamed in Sloan's honor as the Alfred P. Sloan School of Management. In the following decades, the school grew to the point that in 2000, management became the second-largest undergraduate major at MIT. In 2005, an undergraduate minor in management was opened to 100 students each year. In 2014, the school celebrated 100 years of management education at MIT.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Since its founding, the school has initiated many international efforts to improve regional economies and positively shape the future of global business. In the 1960s, the school played a leading role in founding the first [Indian Institute of Management](/wiki/Indian_Institute_of_Management_Calcutta "Indian Institute of Management Calcutta").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Other initiatives include the MIT-China Management Education Project, the International Faculty Fellows Program, and partnerships with [IESE Business School](/wiki/IESE_Business_School "IESE Business School") in Spain, [Sungkyunkwan University](/wiki/Sungkyunkwan_University "Sungkyunkwan University") in [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea"), [Universidade Nova de Lisboa](/wiki/Universidade_Nova_de_Lisboa "Universidade Nova de Lisboa") in [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal"), the [Skolkovo Moscow School of Management](/wiki/Skolkovo_Moscow_School_of_Management "Skolkovo Moscow School of Management") in Russia, and [Tsinghua University](/wiki/Tsinghua_University "Tsinghua University") in China. In 2014, the school launched the MIT Regional Entrepreneurship Acceleration Program (REAP), which brings leaders from developing regions to MIT for two years to improve their economies.[[7]](#cite_note-7) In 2015, MIT worked in collaboration with the [Central Bank of Malaysia](/wiki/Central_Bank_of_Malaysia "Central Bank of Malaysia") to establish [Asia School of Business](/wiki/Asia_School_of_Business "Asia School of Business") in [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur "Kuala Lumpur"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Academics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=MIT_Sloan_School_of_Management&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Academics")]

| Business school rankings | |

The curriculum is focused on action learning, which requires that students apply concepts learned in the classroom to real-world business settings. Courses are taught using the [case method](/wiki/Case_method "Case method"), lectures, team projects, and hands-on Action Learning Labs. The academic level of coursework is considered extremely demanding by business school standards, with a greater emphasis on analytical reasoning and [quantitative analysis](/wiki/Quantitative_analysis_(finance) "Quantitative analysis (finance)") than most programs.[[3]](#cite_note-about-mit-sloan-3)

Academic rigor has a strong influence on the school's culture. The first semester, also known as the core, is often considered the most difficult semester by design. Courses are graded using letter grades and on the standard five-point MIT scale. In its graduate programs, anything less than a 4.0 ('B') average will result in the student not being allowed to graduate. Unlike most business schools, MIT Sloan does not offer any academic honors at graduation, consistent with the practice throughout all of MIT. The philosophy behind this is that the 'honor' is in being an MIT graduate.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

MIT Sloan closely collaborates with other parts of MIT, in particular the [School of Engineering](/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology_School_of_Engineering "Massachusetts Institute of Technology School of Engineering"), the [School of Science](/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology_School_of_Science "Massachusetts Institute of Technology School of Science"), and the [Department of Economics](/wiki/MIT_Department_of_Economics "MIT Department of Economics"). A special joint degree program with the School of Engineering is the [Leaders for Global Operations](/wiki/Leaders_for_Global_Operations "Leaders for Global Operations") (LGO) program, where students concurrently complete an MBA and a Master of Science in engineering.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Another joint degree program aimed at students with more industry experience (an average of 8 to 10 years) is the System Design and Management (SDM) program,[[17]](#cite_note-17) where students complete a Master of Science in Engineering and Management.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/MIT_Building_10_and_the_Great_Dome%2C_Cambridge_MA.jpg/250px-MIT_Building_10_and_the_Great_Dome%2C_Cambridge_MA.jpg)](/wiki/File:MIT_Building_10_and_the_Great_Dome,_Cambridge_MA.jpg)

MIT Sloan degrees are conferred at Killian Court in the Institute-wide commencement.

Creativity and invention are constant themes at the school. The MBA track in *Entrepreneurship & Innovation* features action learning labs which pair students with companies to learn how to solve complex problems relating to emerging technologies. These action learning labs include *Entrepreneurship Lab*, *Innovation Teams*, and *Leading Sustainable Systems Lab*. *Global Entrepreneurship Lab* and *Global Health Delivery Lab* send MBA students to work onsite with startups in different parts of the world. The [Martin Trust Center for MIT Entrepreneurship](/wiki/Martin_Trust_Center_for_MIT_Entrepreneurship "Martin Trust Center for MIT Entrepreneurship"), one of the few business school entrepreneurship centers in the world focused on [high tech](/wiki/High_tech "High tech"), offers many other entrepreneurial activities and mentorship throughout the year.[[18]](#cite_note-eitrack-18) The annual [MIT $100K Entrepreneurship Competition](/wiki/MIT_$100K_Entrepreneurship_Competition "MIT $100K Entrepreneurship Competition") is one of the largest business plan competitions in the world, helping to launch more than 130 companies with a market capitalization of over $15 billion.[[19]](#cite_note-100k-19)

In 2016, the school's MBA program was ranked #2 worldwide for social and environmental impact by *[Corporate Knights](/wiki/Corporate_Knights "Corporate Knights")* magazine.[[20]](#cite_note-Corporate_Knights_MBA_Rankings-20)

## Global offices

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=MIT_Sloan_School_of_Management&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Global offices")]

MIT Sloan operates two global offices, the MIT Sloan Latin America Office (MSLAO) in [Santiago](/wiki/Santiago "Santiago"), [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile"), and the MIT Sloan Office for Southeast Asian Nations (MSAO) in [Bangkok](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok"), [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand").[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) MSLAO opened in 2013, and MSAO is expected to open in October 2024.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Student life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=MIT_Sloan_School_of_Management&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Student life")]

MIT Sloan MBA undergraduate majors

MIT Sloan students and alumni informally call themselves *Sloanies*. The MIT Sloan culture is similar to, but also distinct from, overall [MIT culture](/wiki/Traditions_and_student_activities_at_MIT "Traditions and student activities at MIT"), and is influenced most strongly by its MBA program. MBA students come from more than 60 countries every year, with just over half coming from [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"), and 60% holding US citizenship. Prior to business school, engineering is the most popular undergraduate major among students. 46% of the class is female.[[25]](#cite_note-profile2024-25)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/MIT_Walker_Memorial.jpg/250px-MIT_Walker_Memorial.jpg)](/wiki/File:MIT_Walker_Memorial.jpg)

Walker Memorial is the primary venue for C-Functions and other events.

A staple of MIT Sloan MBA life is the weekly *C-Function*, which stands for "cultural function" or "consumption function". The school sponsors food and drink for all members of the MIT Sloan graduate community to enjoy entertainment organized by specific campus cultural groups or clubs as well as parties with non-cultural themes. C-Functions are usually held most Thursdays in the Walker Memorial building, which is also used as the venue for many other MIT Sloan community events. MIT Sloan alumni groups around the world also organize C-Functions for their club members, for social and networking activities.

Students at MIT Sloan run over 70 active clubs.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Some of the most popular clubs are the *Sloan Women in Management Club*;[[27]](#cite_note-27) the *Entrepreneurship & Innovation Club*; the *Design Club*; the *Finance Club*; the *Management Consulting Club*; the *Entertainment, Media and Sports Club*; the *Venture Capital and Private Equity Club*; the *Product Management Club*;[[28]](#cite_note-28) and the *Technology Club*. The *Sloan Business Club* is the official undergraduate business club for all MIT students.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/2009_MIT_Sloan_Sports_Analytics_Conference.jpg/250px-2009_MIT_Sloan_Sports_Analytics_Conference.jpg)](/wiki/File:2009_MIT_Sloan_Sports_Analytics_Conference.jpg)

[Ray Allen](/wiki/Ray_Allen "Ray Allen") of the [Boston Celtics](/wiki/Boston_Celtics "Boston Celtics") at the [MIT Sloan Sports Analytics Conference](/wiki/MIT_Sloan_Sports_Analytics_Conference "MIT Sloan Sports Analytics Conference")

Throughout the school year, a number of professional and academic conferences are organized by, or in partnership with, the school. Annual highlights include the [MIT $100K Entrepreneurship Competition](/wiki/MIT_$100K_Entrepreneurship_Competition "MIT $100K Entrepreneurship Competition"), the MIT Venture Capital & Innovation Conference, the Sloan Women in Management Breaking the Mold Conference, the MIT Sloan CIO Symposium, and the MIT Sloan CFO Summit. The most visible conference—and the largest student-run conference in the world—is the [MIT Sloan Sports Analytics Conference](/wiki/MIT_Sloan_Sports_Analytics_Conference "MIT Sloan Sports Analytics Conference"), which [*Fast Company*](/wiki/Fast_Company_(magazine) "Fast Company (magazine)") ranked the #3 most innovative sports company, behind only the NFL and [MLB Advanced Media](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_Advanced_Media "Major League Baseball Advanced Media").[[30]](#cite_note-fastco-30)

Like the rest of the institute, MIT Sloan students have an extended period between semesters reserved for special activities. During the month of January, there are no formal classes at the school; instead, they are replaced by what is known as the [Independent Activities Period (IAP)](/wiki/Traditions_and_student_activities_at_MIT#Independent_Activities_Period "Traditions and student activities at MIT"). During IAP, students engage in activities that would be challenging to participate in alongside regular classes, often including international travel programs. In the middle of semesters, the MBA program has an additional, shorter gap, called the Sloan Innovation Period (SIP), focusing on intensive experiential leadership activities outside of the classroom.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

After commencement, MIT Sloan graduates wear the [MIT class ring](/wiki/MIT_class_ring "MIT class ring"), known as the *Brass Rat*. Top recruiters of new MBA graduates of the school include [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc."), [Google](/wiki/Google "Google"), [Goldman Sachs](/wiki/Goldman_Sachs "Goldman Sachs"), [McKinsey & Company](/wiki/McKinsey_%26_Company "McKinsey & Company"), [Nike](/wiki/Nike,_Inc. "Nike, Inc.") and [Amazon](/wiki/Amazon_(company) "Amazon (company)"). The school has over 20,000 alumni globally in 90 countries, with more than 20% who are presidents or CEOs. More than 650 companies have been founded by alumni of the school, including [Akamai](/wiki/Akamai "Akamai"), [E\*Trade](/wiki/E-Trade "E-Trade"), [Gartner](/wiki/Gartner "Gartner"), [Genentech](/wiki/Genentech "Genentech"), [HubSpot](/wiki/HubSpot "HubSpot"), [Lotus Software](/wiki/Lotus_Software "Lotus Software"), [Teradyne](/wiki/Teradyne "Teradyne"), [Zipcar](/wiki/Zipcar "Zipcar"), and [Okta](/wiki/Okta_(identity_management) "Okta (identity management)").[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)
